A-82-0339

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assurance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660         322-5860          322-5591         322-6441         322-6444

March 18, 1982


Robert H. Burnham
Assistant City Attorney
City Hall
3300 Newport Blvd.
Newport Beach, CA  92663

                                             Re: Advice Request
                                                 No. A-82-039

Dear Mr. Burnham:

     Thank you for your letter of March 9, 1982, requesting
advice on behalf of a member of the City of Newport Beach
Planning Commission, Paul Balalis. As I understand it, your
request is based on the following factual situation:

     1)  Mr. Balalis has a 6% ownership interest in the
Newport Harbor National Bank.  He is also the Chairman
of the Board of Directors of the bank.  He receives
more than $250 a year from the bank for his services.

     2)  The Newport Harbor National Bank has made
loans of approximately $240,000 to a private club located
in the City of Newport Beach.  This private club has
applied for and received use permits from the Planning
Commission.

     3)  Mr. Balalis voted on the use permits for this
private club.  Mr. Balalis did not know of the loans at
the time of the votes in question.

     You have asked whether Mr. Balalis' vote on the permits
invalidates the proceedings on the use permits.  You have
also asked whether Mr. Balalis can vote on future decisions
involving this club, or involving the bank's other borrowers
or depositors.

     Under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103,1/ a
public official has to disqualify himself from decisions
which may foreseeably have a material financial effect on a

_________________________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code.

Robert H. Burnham
March 18, 1982
Page Two


source of income, or on a business entity in which the official
has an investment interest. Under the facts you described,
the Newport Harbor National Bank is a source of income to
Mr. Balalis, and he also has an investment interest in the
bank. Thus, any Planning Commission decision which will
foreseeably have a material financial effect on the bank
will present a conflict of interest for Mr. Balalis. In the
case of such a decision, Mr. Balalis must disqualify himself.
However, absent unusual circumstances, a decision affecting
one of the bank's borrowers or depositors will not foreseeably
have a material financial effect on,the bank, and thus will
not present a conflict of interest.^/

   The fact that a decision will affect one of the bank's
borrowers or depositors is not, in itself, sufficient to
create a conflict of interest. Because Mr. Balalis has less
than a 10% interest in the bank, sources of income to the
bank are not sources of income to him (Section 82030), and
investment or real property interests of the bank are not
his investments or interests (Sections 82033, 82034).

   Applying the foregoing analysis, Mr. Balalis need not
disqualify himself from decisions involving the bank's borrowers
or depositors, unless there is some unusual situation in
which the decision will foreseeably affect the bank itself.
Furthermore, Mr. Balalis has no obligation to inform himself
of the identity of the bank's customers.

   I believe that this advice should answer all your questions,
including your first question concerning the validity of the
Planning Commission's actions on the use permits. However,
for future reference, you may want to know that a government
decision is not ordinarily invalidated by the vote or participa-
tion of an official who has a conflict of interest. The
only way such a decision can be invalidated is by a court
order under Section 9100­3(b).

_____________________
^/ There are some decisions regarding a borrower which
might affect the bank financially. For example, a decision
on a permit, when issuance of the permit would determine
whether a borrower would default on a loan, or when issuance
of the permit was a precondition to a loan, would affect the
bank financially. However, such decisions would create a
conflict of interest only if the Planning Commissioner knew
that the loan decision or default was dependent on the permit
decision, and if the potential income to the bank from the
loan or loss from the default met the standards for materiality
set forth in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (copy enclosed).

Robert H. Burnham
March 18, 1982
Page Three


     If you have any further questions, I will be happy to
discuss them with you.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

CITY OF NEWPORT BEACH

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY
714 645-2201


March 9, 1982


Ms. Barbara Milman
Chief Legal Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca. 95804

Re:     Paul Balalis - FPPC Advice Request

Dear Ms. Milman:

This office is requesting advice from the FPPC concerning Paul
Balalis, a member of the Planning Commission for the City of
Newport Beach.

Since January 19, 1982, Mr. Balalis has served as Chairman of the
Board of Directors of Newport Harbor National Bank, and is the
owner of approximately 6% of the outstanding shares of stock
issued by that institution. Prior to taking this position, and
from February 7, 1980, Mr. Balalis was a member of the Board of
Directors of Newport Harbor National Bank.

This letter is prompted by Mr. Balalis' recent disclosure that he
voted on, and participated in the discussions concerning, a Use
Permit Application submitted by a business with which the bank
had placed approximately $240,000.00 in loans. A more thorough
discussion of the relevant facts follows:

Magic Island is the name given to a private club located in the
City of Newport Beach that attracts members by offering top-
quality magic acts, food and a certain ambiance. The business
commenced operation on or about September 4, 1981, approximately
one year after the Planning Commission, with Mr. Balalis' vote
and participation, approved their Use Permit Application. A Use
Permit is a prerequisite to the operation of any restaurant or
recreational establishment within the City of Newport Beach.


City Hall • 3300 Newport Boulevard, Newport Beach, California 92660

Ms. Barbara Milman
March 9, 1982
Page Two

Re:     Paul Balalis - FPPC Advice Request




On February 4, 1982, the Planning Commission considered an
Application by Magic Island to amend the provisions of the pre-
viously granted Use Permit, to allow for daytime use of the
facility as well as expansion of the nighttime operation. The
Planning Commission approved, on a temporary basis, the daytime
use of the facility, and continued the other discretionary
approvals requested by the business.

On or about May 12, 1981, Newport Harbor National Bank placed a
loan in the sum of $200,000.00 with Magic Island, the loan
guaranteed by Michael Callie and Sally Wrainswaite. It is the
understanding of this office that Mr. Callie and Ms. Wrainswaite
are principals in the operation and that both have an ownership
interest. This loan was renewed November 13, 1981.

On or about Auust 26, 1981, a loan in the sum of $100,000.00 was
placed with Michael Callie and secured by real property owned by
Mr. Callie. This loan was paid September 26, 1981.

Each of these loans was made at or above the prime interest rate
in effect at the time and, subject to a determination of credit
worthiness, were available to other businesses or individuals on
the same or similar terms.

Finally, on April 29, 1981, a loan in the sum of $100,000.00 was
made to Michael Callie, which loan was renewed October 30, 1981
and again on January 19, 1982. On or about October 30, 1981, a
loan in the sum of $42,000.00 was placed by Newport Harbor
National Bank, with Magic Island and renewed January 18, 1982.

As we have indicated above, Mr. Balalis is Chairman of the Board
of Directors of Newport Harbor National Bank. The Board, as is
customary, makes policy, with respect to operations, in the broad
sense, and with respect to the manner in which the lending insti-
tution proposes to expand its operations. The Board of Directors
does not approve loans, but after loans are made, the Board of
Directors is given information concerning the size of the loan,
the person or entity with whom the loan was placed and other
relevant information.

Ms. Barbara Milman
March 9, 1982
Page Three

Re:       Paul Balalis - FPPC Advice Request




The process by which loans are placed by the bank is as
follows: Loan applications are presented to a loan officer. The
Loan Officer then reviews the application, acquires all relevant
information, then makes a preliminary determination whether to
approve or deny the loan request. The preliminary determination
by the Loan Officer is then reviewed by a senior Loan Officer, who
has authority to approve loans, up to $420,000.00. Loans to be
placed that are in excess of $420,000.00 are reviewed, and grant-
ed, by a Loan Committee.

Newport Harbor National Bank has approximately 400 loans
outstanding at this time, the total value of which is
approximately $30,000,000.00. The total value of the assets of
the institution is approximately $35,000,000.00.

Mr. Balaliais served on the Loan Committee from February 7, 1980
to March 27, 1981, but has had no involvement with the Loan
Committee since that date. Mr. Balalis has advised this office
that he first became aware of the loan commitments to Magic
Island and principles thereof, on or about February 20, 1982.

As Chairman of the Board, Mr. Balalis receives $250.00 per
meeting, and the Board, customarily, meets once a month. It is
unknown at this time, if, and when, dividends are to be paid on
outstanding shares of stock owned by Mr. Balalis and other share-
holders. One final note, individual Board members who encourage
the placement of loans to persons or entities who are not credit
worthy may be held personally liable for any losses incurred by
the lending institution as a result of the loan.

Your advice is requested on the following questions:

     I.  We have advised the Planning Commission and Mr. Balalis
         that his vote and participation in matters involving
         Magic Island, that occurred prior to the time he became
         aware of the relationship between Magic Island and
         Newport Harbor National Bank, does not invalidate the
         proceedings because, at the time of the participation
         and vote by Mr. Balalis, he did not know, or have reason
         to know, that he had a financial interest in the matter,
         and there has been no challenge to the decision of the
         Planning Commission;

Ms. Barbara Milman
March 9, 1982
Page Four

Re:     Paul Balalis - FPPC Advice Request


III.  Is Mr. Balalais precluded from further participation in
      matters involving Magic Island by virtue of the provis-
      ions of Section 87100 and 87103 A, C and/or D;

III.  Given the size of this lending institution, are there
      any guidelines that would assist Mr. Balaliais in deter-
      mining what size of loan commitment, or other business
      dealing, that will require disqualification;

IV.   Will Mr. Balalalis be precluded from participating in
      matters involving persons who have money on deposit with
      the bank, given the difficult in learning the identity
      of these persons from day-to-day and the fact that
      interest rates on deposits are essentially fixed by the
      Federal Government;

V.    To what extent is Mr. Balalalis obligated to seek
      information as to the identify of borrowers, depositors
      and others who have business dealings with this lending
      institution?

Please contact this office if you are of the opinion that further
information is required to answer somme or all of the questions
set forth above.

Very truly yours,

Robert H. Burnham
Assistant City Attorney

cc.    Jim Hewicker - Planning Director
       Paul Balalis - Planning Commissioner
       Helen McLaughlin - Chairman, Planning Commission


M-2L-Milman-031082

- 5 -

Coastal Dependent Uses

The City of Newport Beach is a unique coastal community with resources

which include the Harbor, beaches, and many types of recreational

facilities. The City is also characterized by waterfront homes and

restaurants, industrial and commercial marine uses, and opportuni-

ties for coastal views. The desireability of the City as a water-

oriented community is also a factor in the desire of non-marine

uses to locate within the City, even where such use is not water

related.

While the Coastal Act does not require a separate component dealing

with coastal-dependent development, such uses are integral to the

character and ambience of the City of Newport Beach. They contri-

bute greatly to the desireability of the City as a regional recrea-

tional, industrial, commercial and financial center. Development

pressures from competing land uses for limited oceanfront and bay-

front resources are being experienced, and it can be assumed that

such pressures will continue to occur.

Considerable diversity exists along the Newport Beach shoreline

with regard to existing land use and character. There is the poten-

tial for the location of new or replacement coastal-dependent and

coastal-related land uses, as well as preservation and enhancement

of existing marine-related uses.

Policies in regards to coastal-dependent development follow:

     1.  Coastal dependent developments shall have priority
          over other developments on or near the shoreline.
          Coastal-dependent uses shall be defined as those
          uses which require a site on or adjacent to the sea
          to be able to function at all.


EXHIBIT A - P3 1

-6-

2. Commercially and industrially zoned areas in the Coastal
Zone shall be designated for coastally-dependent, coast-
ally-related and visitor serving uses as priority uses.
"Coastally-related development" means any use that is
dependent on a coastal-dependent development or use.

3. Facilities which serve commercial fishing shall be
encouraged and maintained unless the demand for the
facilities no longer exist.

4. In view of the City's attractions as a regional and
statewide recreation area, the growing regional and
statewide demand for water-oriented recreational facil-
ities, and the limited capacity of the City's harbor
and oceanfront resources to fully satisfy such demands,
the City shall encourage the opening and development of
adjoining ocean and waterfront areas outside Newport
Bay, so long as any adverse environmental impacts are
mitigated, in a manner which may best serve to distrib-
ute the increasing public need for water-oriented
recreational facilities.

5. The City shall protect and maintain the necessary
support facilities and services for marine recreational
and educational activities inlcuding: Marine ways and
services, launching facilities, gas and pump-out stations,
parking facilities, restrooms, showers, concessions
and educational facilities such as the Sea Scout Base
and the Intercollegiate Rowing Facilities.

6. The City shall protect and enhance public visual access
to the waterfront. New development (including land-
scaping), public or private, shall be sited and designed
to protect public views of ocean and other coastal
scenic areas.

EXHIBIT "A" - P.2

- 7 -

Public Property Leaseholds

Within the City of Newport Beach, there are many publicly-held

lands. Some of these parcels are held in fee by the City. Some

are held by the City as a tidelands trust. Over the years most of

these areas have been leased and uses established on the properties.

While it is not reasonable to expect long-established uses to be

eliminated, the following lease hold policies are established to

guide the leasing and re-leasing of public properties:

1. Any new leasing of public lands for private use shall
be consistent with policies of the California Coastal
Act and shall be subject to voter approval.

2. In the leasing or re-leasing of publicly owned land,
full consideration shall be given to the public's
right of access to the ocean, beach, and bay and
to the provision of coastal-dependent uses adjacent
to the water

3. At the time the leases are negotiated or renewed, care-
ful consideration shall be given to the consistency of
the proposed use with the public interest. If a posi-
tive determination is made, the property shall be
leased at fair market value, unless it is clearly in
the public interest to do otherwise.

4. All new leases and lease renewals of publicly owned
land shall be considered by the City Council at a
public hearing.

EXH1817 "B"

-25-

Policies relating to Visitor Serving Facilities follow:

1. The City shall preserve, in its natural state, the ocean
beaches, waters, surf action, and coastal shoreline in
a manner that will ensure their availability for continued
public use and enjoyment. Proposals for the construction
of anti-erosion structures, off-shore breakwaters, or
future marinas shall be examined in light of this policy.

2. The City shall also preserve and enhance the Upper Bay
and marine coastal preserves which supports varied species
of plant, marine and wildlife populations to ensure their
availability and continued use for ecological, educational
and aesthetic purposes.

3. Consistent with all other policies to protect and enhance
the quality residential character of the community, the
City shall encourage and protect both public and private
water-oriented recreational and entertainment facilities
as a means of providing public access to the waterfront.

4. Provision and maintenance of public restroom facilities
is top priority; the City Council should immediately
commence planning and acquisitions necessary in order that
public restrooms with appropriate signing be constructed
as soon as possible; the size and location of the facilities
shall take the residents into consideration.

4A. That a permanent restroom facility be constructed at West
Jetty Park.

4B. That a permanent restroom facility be constructed in the
vicinity of the Balboa Branch Library.

4C. That a permanent restroom facility be constructed at Las
Arenas Park.

4D. That a permanent restroom facility be constructed between
Orange Avenue and the Santa Ana River.

4E. The City shall commence a signing program to direct the
public to restrooms, access points, and boat launching.

5. The Oceanfront sidewalk shall be extended from "E" Street
to the Wedge.

5A. The Oceanfront sidewalk shall be extended from "36th"
Street to the Santa Ana River.

6. Adequate marine sanitation facilities, including pump out
stations and conveniently located public restrooms in the
Harbor Area, shall be provided in order to reduce the risks
of water pollution and health problems.

EX#11317 "C" D3 1

-26-

7. Drinking fountains shall be provided in conjunction with
restroom facilities as a means of insuring their distri-
bution throughout the Coastal Zone, while minimizing the
costs of extending water lines. Also, exterior showers
shall be provided in conjunction with these facilities.

8. Bicycle racks shall be provided throughout the Coastal
Zone. If available, locking, pay-your-way bicycle racks
shall be used.

9. The City shall encourage and support the provision of
guest slips, mooring and anchorages in Newport Harbor,
and shall continue to work with the Orange County Harbor
Department to provide these facilities where feasible.

10. The City of Newport Beach currently provides overnight
accommodations in all price ranges. The City shall
encourage the maintenance of overnight accommodations
in all price ranges, including encouraging the mainten-
ance of existing summer weekly rentals as a means of
providing overnight accommodations.

11. The City of Newport Beach shall encourage the County of
Orange to provide additional recreational vehicle camp
areas in the Newport Dunes areas as a means of providing
additional overnight accommodations.

12. Development of the lower part of Bayview Landing should
be coordinated with development in Newport Dunes to
offer an expanded recreational vehicle park.

13. The City of Newport Beach shall protect and encourage
the maintenance of lower-cost commercial visitor serving
facilities.

14. The City shall actively pursue the provision of a public
launching facility for small sailboats on lower Newport
Bay along with the required parking.

EXHIBIT "C" - PS 2

-43-

AREA DESCRIPTIONS

Balboa Peninsula Point

Balboa Peninsula Point consists of the residential neighborhood

between "A" Street and the harbor entrance on the Balboa Peninsula.

The area is designated primarily for "Low-Density Residential" with

some areas designated for "Multiple-Family Residential" uses.

The public oceanfront beach and the beach at "N" Street on the

bay have been designated "Recreational and Environmental Open

Space." New proposals for this area include extension of the

oceanfront public bikeway/walkway from "E" Street to The Wedge

and addition of a restroom facility at the West Jetty Park (The

Wedge). The bikeway/walkway shall be designed and located so as

to minimize the impacts on adjacent residential uses (e.g., provide

a buffer between residential uses and sidewalk).

Central Balboa

Central Balboa consists of the commercially-developed area between

"A" Street and Coronado Street. This area is a unique mixture of

visitor-oriented and neighborhood-oriented retail and service uses;

including coastal-dependent visitor uses such as sport fishing

establishments, day-boat rentals, ferry service, fishing docks,

and historic Balboa Pavilion. Those areas which are near the bay

and oceanfronts are shown for "Recreational and Marine Commercial"

uses. Those areas which line East Balboa Boulevard have been

designated "Retail and Service Commercial."

It is the intent of these land use designations to encourage the

continuation of the neighborhood and visitor-serving orientation

EXHIBIT "D"

of the area and to prohibit office uses at the ground level.  In
those areas designated for retail uses, residential and office
uses will be permitted on the second level or above when the
ground level is occupied by a primary use which provides goods or
services directly to the public.

Island Avenue

The Island Avenue area consists of a commercially-developed block
and one block occupied by the Balboa Branch Library and the Balboa
Peninsula Fire Station.  Land use designations reflect the commer-
cial and institutional uses and include a proposal to construct
a restroom facility in the vicinity of the Balboa Branch Library.

Marinapark

Marinapark consists of the City-owned property on Newport Bay
between 15th Street and 19th Street and is designated as "Recrea-
tional and Environmental Open Space."  The existing uses include
a large public beach and boat launching area, buildings used by
the Girl Scouts Voluntary Action Center and American Legion,
public tennis courts, and a mobile home park.  Proposed for this area
is the addition of a restroom facility.  Additionally, the mobile home
park shall be phased out and the property eventually used for public
recreational uses.

McFadden Square

McFadden Square is the commercial area on the ocean side  of Balboa
Boulevard between 19th Street and 24th Street on the Balboa Penin-
sula.  The area consists of commercial use geared mainly to the
needs of beach visitors.  The LCP Land Use Plan designated this

-45-

area for "Recreational and Marine Commercial" uses to maintain the

existing orientation while allowing an upgrading of the area.

Office uses may be permitted on the second level of a structure or

above, where the ground level is occupied by a primary use which

offers goods or services directly to the public. No offices will

be permitted on lots which are directly on the oceanfront.

Cannery Village

Cannery Village is mixed use area on the bay side of Newport

Boulevard between 32nd Street and 28th Street on the Balboa Penin-

sula. The area consists of commercial, industrial and residential

uses; including many of the marine industrial businesses serving

the Newport Harbor Area. The area is highly subject to redevelop-

ment pressures.

In order to maintain the particularly "marine" atmosphere of the

area, close consideration should be given to all proposals for

new development, especially in waterfront areas.

The LCP Land Use Plan designates the waterfront areas "Recreational

and Marine Commercial" a designation which allows all coastally-

dependent commercial and industrial uses. Inland lots are also

designated for "Recreational and Marine Commercial" uses to

encourage a continuation of the marine commercial and specialty

retail uses in the area. On inland lots, offices and residential

uses will be permitted on the second level or above where the

ground floor is occupied by a primary marine commercial or visitor-

serving commercial use.

-46-

McFadden Square Bayfront

This area consists of the area bayward of Balboa Boulevard between

28th Street and 19th Street on the Balboa Peninsula. The area

bayward of Newport Boulevard is designated "Recreational and Marine

Commercial." Those areas between the boulevards are shown for

"Retail and Service Commercial" uses.

Lido Peninsula

The Lido Peninsula is a unique area of the City with a wide variety

of existing and potential uses. It is particularly suited to planned

development concepts if and when substantial changes in existing uses

are undertaken. Meanwhile, all existing uses will be allowed to

continue and be upgraded, but any substantial changes shall be sub-

ject to an approved area plan.

It is desirable that the commercial area in future-planned develop-

ment be consistent with the intent of the "Recreational and Marine

Commercial" designation. Uses permitted include, but are not

limited to, marine repair and service, restaurants, hotels, motels,

specialty shops and offices. Commercial uses shall occupy approx-

imately 30% of the site. The existing commercial marina and

support facilities shall be maintained. The remaining 70% of the

land area shall be devoted to residential uses. The existing

number of mobile home spaces will be maintained by the property

owner either on-site or at another site in the City of Newport Beach.

Lido Isle

No changes in the Lido Isle land use pattern are proposed. All

City-owned beaches have been shown as "Recreational and Environ-

mental Open Space" as well as have the access lots currently

leased back to the Lido Isle Community Association. The City will

establish a signing program to identify the public beaches and the

public access easements.

Lido Village

The Lido Village is that area bayward of Newport Boulevard between

The Arches/Coast Highway Bridge and 32nd Street.  The area is a

mixed retail commercial area shown for "Recreational and Marine

Commercial" on the waterfront and "Retail and Service Commercial"

in other areas.  Newport Beach City Hall is also located in this

area and is shown for "Governmental, Educational and Institutional

Facilities" on the Land Use Plan.  It is proposed that the water-

front walkway be extended in conjunction with any new development

in this area.

32nd Street

The 32nd Street commercial area between Newport Boulevard and

Balboa Boulevard is shown for "Retail and Service Commercial" uses

on the LCP Land Use Plan to encourage the maintenance of these

primarly neighborhood commercial uses.  The vacant land at the

oceanfront end of 32nd Street is designated "Recreational and

Environmental Open Space."

West Newport

All commercial areas on Coast Highway westerly of the Arches-Coast

Highway Bridge are shown for "Retail and Service Commercial" in

order to allow the maintenance and improvement of these commercial

areas which serve residents and visitors alike.  No residential

uses will be permitted in these areas but upper level offices will

be allowed when the ground floor is occupied by a primary use which

offers goods or services to the general public.

-48-

The vacant State Highway Right-of-Way on the south side of Coast

Highway opposite "Newport Shores" will be preserved as "Recreational

and Environmental Open Space," to be used for public recreation,

and visual-environmental purposes. A restroom facility is proposed

in the highway right-of-way westerly of Orange Avenue. Also

proposed for this area is an extension of the public oceanfront

bikeway/walkway from 36th Street to the Santa Ana River. The

bikeway/walkway shall be designed and sited so as to minimize the

impacts on adjacent residential uses.

There is an existing mobile home park on the northerly side of Coast

Highway near the Santa Ana River. The existing number of mobile home

spaces will be maintained by the property owner either on-site or at

another site in the City of Newport Beach. No changes in residential

land use patterns are proposed.

Cal-Trans East

The area between Newport Boulevard and Superior Avenue northerly

of Coast Highway is shown for "Recreational and Environmental Open

Space" for parking, public recreational and visual-environmental

purposes. Expansion of Hoag Hospital facilities may also be

accommodated on the site.

Cal-Trans West

The area westerly of Superior Avenue northerly of Coast Highway

below Newport Crest is designated "Recreational and Environmental

Open Space" with the intent that this parcel be acquired for public

open space.

BEECO Property

That portion of the BEECO property within the City boundaries is

shown for "Low-Density Residential" uses at four dwelling units per

buildable acre.

-49-

Mariners' Mile

The commercial area along Coast Highway known as "Mariners' Mile"

has been designated as a "Specific Area Plan" area, and a plan has

been adopted for the area. The land use patterns shown for the

area on the LCP reflects generally this prior planning. Areas

northerly of Coast Highway are shown for "Retail and Service

Commercial." Most areas southerly of Coast Highway have been

designated "Recreational and Marine Commercial" in order to pre-

serve existing and encourage new marine uses. The Sea Scout Base

and Intercollegiate Rowing Base have been shown for "Governmental,

Educational and Institutional Facilities." There is an existing mobile

home park behind the post office in Mariners Mile. The existing

number of mobile home spaces will be maintained by the property owner

either on-site or at another site in the City of Newport Beach.

Balboa Bay Club

This City-owned parcel is currently developed with many uses,

including recreational, boating, restaurant and banquet, and resi-

dential facilities, and is leased to the Balboa Bay Club, Inc. The

site is designated for "Recreational and Marine Commercial" uses

on the LCP Land Use Plan. At such time as the extension of the

lease is negotiated, full consideration shall be given to public

access to the site.

Castaways

The southerly five acres of this site are designated "Retail and

Service Commercial," with no hotels or motels permitted. No change

to the existing church site is contemplated so it is shown for

"Governmental, Educational and Institutional Facilities." The

remaining sixty acres are designated "Low-Density Residential" at

4 DU's per buildable acre. A public bikeway/walkway is to be

provided in the blufftop setback area with access from Dover Drive

and/or Westcliff Drive.

The area adjacent to the New Coast Highway bridge at Dover Drive is

currently developed as a mobile home park. The existing number of

mobile home spaces shall be maintained by the property owner either

on-site or another site in the City of Newport Beach.

North Star Beach

The area known as "North Star Beach" is shown for "Recreational and

Environmental Open Space" on the LCP Land Use Plan. It is desirable

that the site be improved to provide a higher quality beach. Also,

any signs which label the beach as private shall be removed and

appropriate signs showing the public nature of the area be provided.

Westbay

The Westbay site is a large vacant parcel adjacent to the Upper

Newport Bay Ecological Reserve at Irvine Avenue. The area is

designated on the Newport Beach General Plan for "Low-Density Resi-

dential" uses at 4 DU's per buildable acre with 75% of the allowable

units transferred to Newport Center.

Since a substantial portion of the allowable development has been

Coastal Dependent Uses 
The City of Newport Beach is a unique coastal community with resources 
which include the Harbor, beaches, and many types of recreational 
facilities. 
The City is al~o characterized by waterfront homes and 
restaurants s industrial and commercial marine uses s and opportuni-
ties for coastal views. 
The desireability of the City as a water-
oriented community is also a factor in the desire of non-marine 
uses to locate within the Citys even where such use is not water 
related. 
While the Coastal Act does not require a separate component dealing 
with coastal-dependent development, such uses are integral to the 
I 
character and ambience of the City of Newport Beach. 
They contri-
bute greatly to the desireability of the City as a regional recrea-
II 
tional s industrial, commercial and financial center. 
Development 
I 
I 
pressures from competing land uses for limited oceanfront and bay-
front resources are being experienced, and it can be assumed that 
such pressures will continue to occur. 
Considerable diversity exists along the Newport Beach shoreline 
I 
with regard to existing land use and character. 
There is the poten-
I 
I 
I

tial for the location of new or replacement coastal-dependent and 
coastal-related land uses, as well as preservation and enhancement 
of existing marine-related uses. 
Policies in regards to coastal-dependent development follow: 
1. 
Coastal dependent developments shall have priority 
over other developments on or near the shoreline. 
Coastally-dependent uses shall be defined as those 
uses which require a site on or adjacent to the sea 
to be able to function at all.

ll means any use that is 
dependent on a coastal-dependent development or use. 
3. 
Facilities which serve commercial fishing shall be 
encouraged and maintained unless the demand for the 
facilities no longer exist. 
4. 
In view of the City's attractions as a regional and 
statewide recreation area, the growing regional and 
statewide demand for water-oriented recreational facil-
ities, and the limited capacity of the City's harbor 
and oceanfront resources to fully satisfy such demands, 
the City shall encourage the opening and development of 
adjoining ocean and waterfront areas outside Newport 
Bay, so long as any adverse environmental impacts are 
mitigated, in a manner which may best serve to distri-
bute the increasing public need for water-oriented 
recreational facilities. 
5. 
The City shall protect and maintain the necessary 
support facilities and services for marine recreational 
and educational activities inlcuding: 
Marine ways and 
services, launching facilities, gas and pump-out stations, 
parking facilities, restrooms, showers, concessions 
and educational facilities such as the Sea Scout Base 
and the Intercollegiate Rowing Facilities. 
6. 
The City shall protect and enhance public visual access 
to the waterfront. 
New development (including land-
scaping), public or private, shall be sited and designed 
to protect public views of ocean and other coastal 
scenic areas.

Public Property Leaseholds 
Within the City of Newport Beach. there are many publicly-held 
lands. 
Some of these parcels are held in fee by the City. 
Some 
are held by the City as a tidelands trust. 
Over the years most of 
these areas have been leased and uses established on the properties. 
While it is not reasonable to expect long-established uses to be 
eliminated, the following lease hold policies are established to 
guide the leasing and re-leasing of public properties~ 
1. 
Any new leasing of public lands for private use shall 
be consistent with policies of the California Coastal 
Act and shall be subject to voter approval. 
2. 
In the leasing or re-leasing of publicly owned land, 
full consideration shall be given to the public's 
right of access to the ocean, beach. and bay and 
to the provision of coastal-dependent uses adjacent 
to the water 
3. 
At the time the leases are negotiated or renewed, care-
ful consideration shall be given to the consistency of 
the proposed use with the public interest. 
If a posi-
tive determination is made, the property shall be 
leased at fair market value, unless it is clearly in 
the public interest to do otherwise. 
4. 
All new leases and lease renewals of publicly owned 
land shall be considered by the City Council at a 
public hearing.

1. 
The City shall preserve, in its natural state, the ocean 
beaches, water, surf action, and coastal shoreline in 
a manner that will ensure their availability for continued 
public use and enjoyment.

either Newport Center or North Ford at the option of the property 
owner. 
The structures shall be clustered to accommodate archaeo-
logical sites and marsh sites. 
A public bikeway/walkway is proposed 


- 52-
for this site. 
Newporter Inn 
The Newporter Inn site has been designated for "Recreational and 
r~arine Commercial ll uses in order to promote the continuation of the 
hotel use and allow expansion if needed. 
Newport Center 
Approximately one-third of the Newport Center site falls within 
the Coastal Zone. 
Most of the area is occupied by the Irvine 
Coast Country Club, shown as IIRecreational and Environmental Open 
Space ll on the Land Use Plan. 
The Marriott Hotel site is designated 
for IIAdministrative, Professional and Financial Commercial
ll uses 
to reflect the hotel use on the site. 
Permitted office uses on 
Corporate Plaza West and the Chamber of Commerce sites are shown 
by the IIAdministrative, Professional and Financial Commercial
ll 
designation, and residential uses are shown on the Sea Island and 
Coast Highway/Jamboree sites by the IIMedium Density Residential!! 
designation. 
Bayview Landing. 
This site adjacent to the Newport Dunes area is designated IIRecrea-
tional and Environmental Open Space 'l for public recreation uses with 
a view park and bike path on the upper portion, and R-V camping on 
the lower portion or similar recreational uses of no greater 
intensity. 
As an alternate use, 4 DU's per buildable acre will be 
permitted to be located on the lower portion of the site to preserve 
existing public views. 
I' 


I 
De Anza Trailer Park 
The De Anza Trailer Park is shown for "Multi-Family Residential" 
uses. 
It is the intent of the City to preserve the trailer park 
use as a means of providing a variety of housing opportunities 
within the City. 
The existing number of mobile home spaces shall 
be maintained by the property owner wither on-site or at another 
site in the City of Newport Beach. 
Beacon Bay 
The Beacon Bay area is a City-owned parcel which is currently 
leased and used for both residential and marine commercial uses. 
The LCP Land Use Plan designations reflect existing uses. 
It is 
the intent of the land use designations to preserve the existing 
land use pattern, particularly the marine commercial uses in the 
Balboa Yacht Basin Area. 
Coast Highwal/Bayside Drive 
This site is currently developed with three major restaurants (the 
Reuben E. Lee, Reuben's, and Isadore's), as well as a yacht broker 
and parking for the docking facilities. 
This area has been desig-
nated for "Recreational and Marine Commercial." 
In addition the 
area to be vacated upon completion of the Newport Bay bridge, shall 
be utilized for a boat launching facility. 
Bayside and Marine 
This vacant site at the entrance to Balboa Island is shown for 
"Recreational and Ma.rine Commercial." 
It is the intent of this 
designation to provide an appropriate land use to be consistent 
with the existing project approved for the site. 
If the office 
project approved for the site cannot be developed, every effort 
shall be made to acquire the site for public open space. 


- 54-
Balboa Island 
No changes to the existing land use pattern on Balboa Island are 
proposed. 
The Marine Avenue and Agate Avenue commercial districts 
are shown for "Retail and Service Commercial" to encourage contin-
uation of the existing patterns. 
Office and residential uses shall 
be prohibited on the ground level, but may be allowed on the second 
level where the ground level is occupied by a primary use which 
provides goods or services directly to the public. 
The submerged 
areas utilized by the Ferry Landing and fuel docks have been 
designated "Recreational and Marine Commercial." 
Only coastally-
dependent uses will be permitted on these bay sites. 
Bayside Drive 
The commercial areas along Bayside Drive below Irvine Terrace are 
shown for "Recreational and Marine Commercial" uses allowing con-
tinuation of the yacht clubs and the Orange County Harbor Depart-
ment facility. 
If redevelopment should occur, uses shall be limited 
to those allowed by this designation. 
Corona del Mar/Coast Highway Commercial 
The Corona del Mar commercial area along Coast Highway is shown for 
"Retail and Service Commercial" uses. 
This area functions primarily 
as a neighborhood commercial area and no changes are being proposed. 
Upper level offices may be permitted consistent with the Retail and 
Service Commercial designation. 
£orona del Mar State Beach 
The Corona del Mar State Beach is a primary visitor destination 


\'/ i t h i nth e Cit y 0 f New p 0 r t B e a c h • 
The des i g nat ion for 
II R e c rea t ion a 1 
and Environmental Open Space" is consistent with the existing use. 
Inspiration Point 
The existing publicly-held parcels, as well as the two undeveloped 
private parcels at Inspiration Point, have been shown for "Recrea-
tional and Environmental Open Space." 
These bluffs constitute a 
resource ~ State-wide significance and as such it is proposed that 
any privately-held vacant parcels in this area be acquired with 
State Coastal Conservancy Funds in order that they be preserved 
for the enjoyment of the public. 
Newport Beach Marine Life Refuge 
The Newport Beach Marine Li fe Refuge is shown for "Recreational and 
Environmental Open Space." 
The purpose of the Marine Life Refuge 
r 
is to conserve the natural flora and fauna resources of the shore-
I 
line, and to protect the marine life in the tide pool areas. 
I 
Buck Gully/Morning Canyon 
The Buck Gully and Morni ng Canyon areas have been shown for"Recrea-
tional and Envi ronmental Open Space" to be preserved in a natural 
state. 
It is proposed that the setbacks of residential lots abut-
ting these areas be adjusted to prevent alteration of the natural 
canyons. 
Upper Newport Bay Ecological Reserve 
This area is designated for "Recreational and Environmental Open 
Space," with detailed plans to be developed by the State Depart-
ment of Fish and Game. 


LAND USE MAPS 
The Land Use Maps which follow illustrate the proposed LPC Land 
Use designations for the Coastal Zone portion of the City. 
The 
City's existing zoning maps are used as a base, with the proposed 
LCP designation as an overlay. 
The existing zoning is illustrated 
with letters (e.g., R-l, R-2, C-l, etc.) and the proposed LCP 
designation is shown with overlay patterns indexed at the bottom 
of each map. 
Existing land use is not shown. 
In many cases the proposed LCP Land Use will not be consistent with 
respect to the LCP zoning regulations when adopted, and would 
probably require Use Permit review for any proposed expansion where 
inconsistencies exist. 
The City's existing zoning ordinance has 
similar provisions for review of nonconforming uses when expanded.

sections.question_synthetic: Can a Planning Commission member who is also a board chairman of a bank participate in matters involving a business that has loans from that bank, and to what extent must he disqualify himself under Government Code sections 87100 and 87103?
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: This is an incoming request letter (from city attorney to FPPC), not an FPPC response. No conclusion section present. Document includes some unrelated content about coastal-dependent uses and public property leaseholds that appears to be from a different document (poor OCR or scanning artifact). Five specific legal questions enumerated but marked as question I-V in the letter itself.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: I. We have advised the Planning Commission and Mr. Balalis that his vote and participation in matters involving Magic Island, that occurred prior to the time he became aware of the relationship between Magic Island and Newport Harbor National Bank, does not invalidate the proceedings because, at the time of the participation and vote by Mr. Balalis, he did not know, or have reason to know, that he had a financial interest in the matter, and there has been no challenge to the decision of the Planning Commission; II. Is Mr. Balalais precluded from further participation in matters involving Magic Island by virtue of the provisions of Section 87100 and 87103 A, C and/or D; III. He receives more than $250 a year from the bank for his services. 2) The Newport Harbor National Bank has made loans of approximately $240,000 to a private club located in the City of Newport Beach. This private club has applied for and received use permits from the Planning Commission. 3) Mr. Balalis voted on the use permits for this private club. Mr. Balalis did not know of the loans at the time of the votes in question. You have asked whether Mr. Balalis' vote on the permits invalidates the proceedings on the use permits. You have also asked whether Mr. Balalis can vote on future decisions involving this club, or involving the bank's other borrowers or depositors. Under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103,1/ a public official has to disqualify himself from decisions which may foreseeably have a material financial effect on a _________________________ 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code. Robert H. Burnham March 18, 1982 Page Two source of income, or on a business entity in which the official has an investment interest. Under the facts you described, the Newport Harbor National Bank is a source of income to Mr. Balalis, and he also has an investment interest in the bank. Thus, any Planning Commission decision which will foreseeably have a material financial effect on the bank will present a conflict of interest for Mr. Balalis. In the case of such a decision, Mr. Balalis must disqualify himself. However, absent unusual circumstances, a decision affecting one of the bank's borrowers or depositors will not foreseeably have a material financial effect on,the bank, and thus will not present a conflict of interest.^/ The fact that a decision will affect one of the bank's borrowers or depositors is not, in itself, sufficient to create a conflict of interest. Because Mr. Balalis has less than a 10% interest in the bank, sources of income to the bank are not sources of income to him (Section 82030), and investment or real property interests of the bank are not his investments or interests (Sections 82033, 82034).
